summary: Gruber argues that Adobe's Flash dominance on the web is being undermined by Apple's refusal to support it on iPhone OS devices, and that web producers will pragmatically adapt rather than wait for Apple to change course. He builds on Robert Scoble's analogy comparing the situation to Firefox forcing sites to abandon IE-specific code. Gruber systematically eliminates possible resolutions to the 'blue boxes' problem, concluding that only web site producers can fix it by providing Flash alternatives. He points out that many sites Flash evangelist Lee Brimelow cited as broken already work fine on iPhone, undermining Adobe's narrative. Adobe's claim of 99% Flash penetration is misleading because it excludes the very mobile devices driving the debate. The piece concludes that practical business interests, not loyalty to Adobe, will drive the transition away from Flash.
key_points: Of the four parties who could resolve Flash's absence on iPhone OS, only web producers have both the incentive and ability to act
Adobe's 99% Flash penetration statistic is misleading because it only counts PCs, excluding the mobile devices at the center of the debate
Many sites cited by Adobe's Flash evangelist as broken without Flash already have working iPhone-optimized versions
Flash's value proposition depended on true ubiquity — 'almost everywhere' is fundamentally different from 'everywhere'
Web producers are pragmatists who follow users, not platform loyalists — they'll adapt to reach iPhone OS users
money_quotes: There's a big difference between "everywhere" and "almost everywhere".
Adobe is arguing that Flash is installed on 99 percent of all web browsers that support Flash, not 99 percent of all web browsers.
Hulu isn't a Flash site, it's a video site. Developers go where the users are.
Used to be you could argue that Flash, whatever its merits, delivered content to the entire audience you cared about. That's no longer true.
Web site producers tend to be practical. Those that use Flash do so not because they're Flash proponents, but because Flash is easy and ubiquitous.
Adobe's Flash penetration is shrinking with each iPhone OS device Apple sells.
What's Hulu going to do? Sit there and wait? Whine about the blue boxes? Or do the practical thing and write software that delivers video to iPhone OS?
key_insight: Flash's dominance depended on true ubiquity, and once Apple broke that by excluding it from iPhone OS, the rational response from web producers was inevitable: adapt to reach users, not defend Adobe's platform.
